responseSet.title: Clinical Infant Reports. Series of the National Centre for Clinical Infant Programs. Number 3. Infants in Multirisk Families. Case Studies in Preventive Intervention. Edited by Stanley I. Greenspan. Maddison, Conn: Int. Univ. Press. $40.00.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Infant Reports. Series of the National Centre for Clinical Infant Programs. Number 3. Infants in Multirisk Families. Case Studies in Preventive Intervention. Edited by Stanley I. Greenspan. Maddison, Conn: Int. Univ. Press. $40.00.

Robin Anderson

Robin Anderson

Robin Anderson  

This book, as the title implies, is likely to be mainly of relevance to those with a special interest in multi-problem families.

It is produced by the Clinical Infant Development Program under the auspices of the National Institute of Mental Health (USA) and is in the nature of a social programme for multi-problem families involving day-care and intensive psychosocial provision by a multidisciplinary team ranging from social workers to psychologists and psychiatrists. This is all observed in very great detail, together with the social context in which the families live. Its scope is in many ways huge. It is attempting to provide information about what kinds of provision and intervention have the best chance of reversing the cycle of deprivation. An important hypothesis is that the maladaptive patterns begin in infancy, and any attempt to reverse the cycle must begin at least during the early months of life, and preferably by identifying and responding to ‘at-risk’ mothers when they are pregnant. For this reason, the programme is directed at families with young children, most of which consist of single mothers with transient and changing partners.

While the study is not primarily psychoanalytic it is quite sympathetic to psychoanalytic ideas, and uses them in conceptualising psychopathology

Part of the programme consists in attempting to engage the mothers by a particular worker in a psychotherapeutic relationship using a modified psychoanalytic approach. Initially these very deprived mothers are interested only in what they can concretely get out of the therapist, and the therapist is responsive to this, giving extra analytic help and being available outside session times. Gradually the mothers come to perceive an emotional need, and in many cases it was possible to develop a relationship which more resembled formal psychotherapy, in which the transference developed and could be interpreted with resulting development of greater insight by the mothers, which certainly appeared to allow some of them to make quite fundamental changes in their lives.

responseSet.documentRef: Roberts, B. (1988) The Shoemaker, The Anatomy of a Psychotic by Flora Rheta Schreiber. Penguin.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:183-185

responseSet.title: The Shoemaker, The Anatomy of a Psychotic by Flora Rheta Schreiber. Penguin.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Shoemaker, The Anatomy of a Psychotic by Flora Rheta Schreiber. Penguin.

Bernard Roberts

Bernard Roberts

Bernard Roberts  

This book is about three issues. It is the result of Professor Schreiber's painstaking and sympathetic research, including long interviews with Joseph Michael Kallinger, an infamous and chronically psychotic murderer. Kallinger was convicted in 1977 of several murders and subsequently imprisoned for life. It is in one way a personal history of Kallinger and the development of his paranoid psychosis. It is also an attempt at a detailed analysis of savage torture and murdering, including a sometimes-gruesome exposition of the crimes which Professor Schreiber explains on the basis of the exceptional cruelty shown to Kallinger by his adoptive parents.

Finally, the book graphically portrays how, by getting caught up in a moralistic attitude about whether Kallinger was mad or bad, the professionals failed to recognize the gravity of his psychosis. Kallinger was detained for assaulting his daughter but his dangerousness was underestimated and he was freed. It was after this that he murdered. Even at his trial his psychosis was missed and it was only on hospitalization following an inexplicable murderous assault towards another prisoner that his paranoid delusions were recognized and appropriate treatment administered.

Joseph Kallinger was the second illegitimate child of a deprived Jewish mother.

183

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

After five years as Founding Editor of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, Michael Sinason is stepping aside. The dedication and skill needed to start a new journal is enormous, and the APP was fortunate to find in Dr Sinason someone who had both experience in publishing and who was a respected psychoanalyst and psychotherapist. Under his guidance Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy is now a well established and respected Journal.

I am very pleased to have the opportunity of becoming the new Editor and very mindful of the responsibility this places on me.

These are difficult and challenging times for everyone working in the National Health Service and other ‘public sector’ institutions. It is ironic when a recent Radio 4 documentary on psychotherapy could speak of the ‘psychotherapy revolution’ and of the ‘veritable mushrooming’ of interest in psychotherapy that so many psychotherapy services, some with international reputations, should be threatened with closure or severe cuts.

Often psychotherapy and related services are having to compete with the more dramatic demands of medicine and surgery; and professionals frequently find it hard to feel they are fighting for equally valuable and valid causes. These battles will continue to be fought locally and nationally, and the APP will continue to take a leading role in these campaigns.

I think it is more important than ever that we should be protecting not only our own work but the standards of our work. I believe the most important function of a Journal such as this is to protect and develop the standard of discourse of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. John Steiner (personal communication) has described the scientific basis of the field of psychotherapy as ‘soft’ because our data is linked to such personal attributes as feelings. We feel very vulnerable when we have to stand up for ourselves against those using so-called ‘hard’ data, which can be statistically verified. And yet we also know that if we try to move towards the ‘hard sciences’ too far, then all we do is abandon a proper consideration of the complexities and subtleties of the human mind, and the psychoanalytic method of studying it. On the other hand, we can play into the hands of those who consider us woolly and unscientific if we use the need to present our data in a meaningful way to hide confusion and woolliness. Our special problem in thinking about and describing our work calls for a greater level of observation and discussion if we are to feel properly rooted in our work, both individually and collectively.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Adopted Child in Analysis

Iain Dresser

Iain Dresser

27a Priory Terrace
London NW6 4DG

Iain Dresser  

In this paper I shall describe something of the analysis of a 12-year-old boy who was adopted at the age of 3 1/2 months, and I hope to be able to illustrate some of the difficulties that may arise in adoption. I shall describe the overall picture of adoption in this country, and how it has changed.

responseSet.documentRef: Cookson, A. (1988) Images of Art Therapy: New Developments in Theory and Practice by Tessa Dalley et al. Pp. 221. Tavistock. £29.50 hardback; £14.95 paperback.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:278-280
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Cookson, A. (1988) Images of Art Therapy: New Developments in Theory and Practice by Tessa Dalley et al. Pp. 221. Tavistock. £29.50 hardback; £14.95 paperback.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:278-280
responseSet.documentRefXML: Cookson, A. (1988) Images of Art Therapy: New Developments in Theory and Practice by Tessa Dalley et al. Pp. 221. Tavistock. £29.50 hardback; £14.95 paperback.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:278-280
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1988 3 3 278-280 Images of Art Therapy: New Developments in Theory and Practice by Tessa Dalley et al. Pp. 221. Tavistock. £29.50 hardback; £14.95 paperback. Alison Cookson
responseSet.title: Images of Art Therapy: New Developments in Theory and Practice by Tessa Dalley et al. Pp. 221. Tavistock. £29.50 hardback; £14.95 paperback.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Images of Art Therapy: New Developments in Theory and Practice by Tessa Dalley et al. Pp. 221. Tavistock. £29.50 hardback; £14.95 paperback.

Alison Cookson

Alison Cookson

Alison Cookson  

Whilst this is undoubtedly a specialist book, in so far as it is representative of current thought and practice in art therapy, it is also of relevance to those psychotherapists wh have an interest in non-verbal forms of expression and communication.

Written by a group of seven women, each of whom is an artist, a practitioner and teacher of art therapy, its evolution into book form arose out of two years of meeting together to discuss clinical and professional issues. Each chapter reflects its author's individual standpoint and this makes for a collection of writings which stimulates and tantalises through the glimpses it offers of diverse and complex ideas about art therapy.

Diane Waller, in the final chapter of the book, which is in part concerned with the development of art therapy into a recognised profession, suggests that it is only when such an identity has become securely established, that it becomes possible to enter into an open dialogue concerning the underlying theoretical tenets upon which such a profession is founded. The differences of opinion concerning the balance and tension between Art and Therapy is a live issue within the profession and is addressed by the co-authors of this book.

Dalley opens up this issue in the first chapter by considering the ways in which art therapists’ attitudes influence the centrality of the image to the process of art therapy and she notes the uneasy tensions often present when attempting to reconcile the art process with the process of psychotherapy. Some art therapists are very concerned that there are those who are taking the “art out of art therapy” (p. 3) and that the link between art and psychotherapy is too strong, resulting in a form of “illustrative” (p. 3) therapy in which the patient makes a picutre which may be used only as a vehicle to enhance verbal communication with the therapist. The fear that something of inherent value is lost if too much emphasis is placed on the use of language brings into question how one might understand the process of creating an image and subsequently how that image might be utilised in the relationship between patient and art therapist. Dalley points out that the process of image-making is intimately connected with creativity, which itself originates from primary processes. She notes that Freud bypassed the integrative and self-enhancing aspects of creativity through a concentration on the art product and the links he made between it, the psychological material it contained and its infantile roots within the psyche of the artist. It was Jung who emphasised the healing qualities of the creative process and its ability to effect a synthesis between inner and outer reality which, as Dalley says, can be embodied in the image and integrated in such a way as to ‘give a sense of reconciliation and resolution’ (p. 9).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Politics of Mental Handicap by Joanna Ryan and Frank Thomas Revised edition. London: Free Association Books. Pp. 189. £5.95. paperback.

Gregory O'brien

Gregory O'brien

Gregory O'brien  

This is the second edition of this book, and it is most interesting to look back at the reviews which heralded the first edition. At that time, it was regarded as a major landmark in the history of our thinking about such issues as how mentally handicapped people should be regarded on a personal, interpersonal and societal level. Reviewing the book now, it is rather more like a timebox, containing the relics and thoughts of a bygone age, the results of which we now see around us in the services which have since evolved, or rather, devolved.

That being said, I found myself splitting the book into the good, the bad and the interesting.

Much of ‘the good’ is now mainline thinking in mental-handicap practice. However, the reader new to this area may find the explanations of such important phenomena as labelling, rejection, and society's need to regard mentally-handicapped people as different, useful.

280

responseSet.title: “Doing the Splits”: Psychotherapy in a Residential Institution for Psychotic Children
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Doing the Splits”: Psychotherapy in a Residential Institution for Psychotic Children

Adrian Sutton

Adrian Sutton

Walmer Street
Manchester, M14 5NP

Adrian Sutton  

Conducting psychotherapy in a residential institution introduces complications not usually encountered in out-patient work. A description is given of the institution, of the position of the therapist who also had other roles in the institution, and of the patient, a girl of 11 years old with a diagnosis of childhood psychosis. Complications which arose for both patient and therapist in relation to each other and the institution, and their responses to these are described.

It is concluded that it was possible to develop and maintain the relationship necessary for psychoanalytic psychotherapy in a residential institution where both parties had relationships with other staff and children. It creates problems which require new responses to be developed by patient and therapist. The position of the psychotherapy in the overall treatment of the child is discussed and it is suggested that sharing more information with other staff than is usual in psychotherapy can be beneficial for the child.

responseSet.documentRef: Parsons, M. (1988) Forty-Two Lives in Treatment: A Study of Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy by Robert S. Wallerstein. Pp 784. £39. Guilford.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:181-183
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Parsons, M. (1988) Forty-Two Lives in Treatment: A Study of Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy by Robert S. Wallerstein. Pp 784. £39. Guilford.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:181-183

responseSet.title: Forty-Two Lives in Treatment: A Study of Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy by Robert S. Wallerstein. Pp 784. £39. Guilford.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Forty-Two Lives in Treatment: A Study of Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy by Robert S. Wallerstein. Pp 784. £39. Guilford.

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons  

This book is a final overview of the Menninger Foundation Psychotherapy Research Project (PRP). It is an extraordinary document, not surprisingly since it is the record of a unique piece of research carried out in unique circumstances.

The Menninger Foundation consists of a full-scale psychiatric hospital with in-patient and out-patient facilities and varieties of intermediate care, with a research establishment attached, entirely devoted to psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Around 1950 a project was conceived for learning more about its work by examining in as much detail as possible the treatment of a group of patients and its result, from initial assessment, through their therapy or analysis to its termination, and on into the subsequent course of their lives. Of cardinal importance was the decision to make it a ‘naturalistic’ research project: to observe and assess the treatment as it was ordinarily conducted, with no variation of normal procedures for methodological reasons. So completely was this principle observed that neither patients nor therapists knew that they were included in such a study until the treatment came to its natural ending, at which point they were interviewed by the researchers.

This secrecy, ethically unacceptable today, is only one of many considerations which mean this study cannot be replicated. Indeed the book is historically fascinating for the incidental ways it reflects an attitude to psychoanalytic treatment in the therapeutic culture of the US in the 1950s. I doubt if whole families are so ready nowadays to uproot themselves and settle for years in a different part of the country, so that somebody can have psychoanalysis. The Menninger Foundation is the only place of its kind, of course, and a very prestigious place to be treated. Even so, one gets clues to the idealisation of analytic treatment, not in the investigators so much as in American society at large, which is being paid for in its relative derogation today.

responseSet.title: The Concept of Mourning and its Roots in Infancy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Mourning and its Roots in Infancy

Victoria Hamilton

Victoria Hamilton

London NW3 5BA

Victoria Hamilton  

Since Freud's seminal paper ‘Mourning and Melancholia’, the term mourning has become a common psychoanalytic concept. As such, it carries many of its ordinary connotations relating to death and the endings encountered in everyday life. In addition, the term has acquired a more specialised meaning in which it is used to donote complex developmental processes in infancy. In this paper, I draw various distinctions between the events of endings and the mental processes — beliefs and feelings — associated with these events. I discuss the pivotal place occupied by the belief in reunion in mourning and some aspects of the distinction between separation and separateness. I point to the contexts other than conflict and pain in which a sense of separateness can develop. The literature on mourning necessarily focusses upon the pains of separation and separateness. A clinical example is given in which an infant's rocking is viewed as a compromise or ‘symptom’ of disordered mourning. The rocking is understood as the best way in which a mother and baby cope with the pains of separation and separateness.

responseSet.title: Are Incest Victims Hated?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Are Incest Victims Hated?

Brendan MaCcarthy

Brendan MaCcarthy

44 Fitzjohns Avenue
London NW3 5LX

Brendan MaCcarthy  

The incest victim often fears that discolure will provoke a hostile environmental response, expressed as disbelief, blame, indifference or outright hostility. This expectation contributes to the decision not to disclose, for years, or perhaps for ever.

The consequence of disclosure frequently bear out the victim's fears within the family, in society generally, within the legal process, and in health and welfare responses.

The paper examines the dynamics of this situation, with regard to the countertransference clues of which the author became aware during the intensive psychoanalytical treatment of incest victims.

responseSet.documentRef: Atkinson, J. (1988) A Psychology with a Soul by Jean Hardy. Routledge. Pp. 245. £6.95.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:179-180
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Atkinson, J. (1988) A Psychology with a Soul by Jean Hardy. Routledge. Pp. 245. £6.95.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:179-180
responseSet.documentRefXML: Atkinson, J. (1988) A Psychology with a Soul by Jean Hardy. Routledge. Pp. 245. £6.95.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:179-180
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1988 3 2 179-180 A Psychology with a Soul by Jean Hardy. Routledge. Pp. 245. £6.95. Jim Atkinson
responseSet.title: A Psychology with a Soul by Jean Hardy. Routledge. Pp. 245. £6.95.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychology with a Soul by Jean Hardy. Routledge. Pp. 245. £6.95.

Jim Atkinson

Jim Atkinson

Jim Atkinson  

Jean Hardy's purpose in writing this book is to establish the credibility of psychosynthesis and its originator Roberto Assagioli. He, like Freud and Jung, was a psychiatrist who considered himself a scientist and pragmatist. Also, like Jung, he was essentially a mystic and healer concerned with the struggles of the soul, the self or the spiritual aspect of the individual.

The book opens with a description of the history, and an explanation of the concepts of psychosynthesis which have an idealised grandiose quality expressed in terms like the Higher Self, for the Soul, transcendent self-actualizer, and the notion of people as basically without evil. The importance of symbols as the language of the unconscious is mentioned, and the process of integration of unconscious content through synthesis is described. This is similar to the notion of the transcendent function in analytical psychology. Indeed there are many similarities to Jung's psychology in Hardy's description of psychosynthesis. Another example is the stimulations of imaginative processes through what Jung terms active imagination. Psychosynthesis apparently uses more active techniques to exercise imagination and borrows freely from many other disciplines, for instance guided fantasy from Gestalt psychology.

179

responseSet.title: The Use of Psychodynamics in Understanding Elderly in-Patients
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Use of Psychodynamics in Understanding Elderly in-Patients

Linda Treliving

Linda Treliving

Dundee, DD2 5NF

Linda Treliving  

This paper is a descriptive account of phenomena observed whilst working as a psychiatric Registrar as part of the team responsible for the admission and assessment of patients over the age of 65 years suffering from a functional psychiatric illness.

Amongst the issues described are the difficulties faced by young psychiatrists in dealing with this group of patients who are, in one way or another, grappling with the tasks of growing old and facing death.

The concepts of loneliness and regression have been outlined with particular reference to their contributions in the normal ageing process the symptomatology of illness in the elderly and, again, the effects these may have on the care-givers.

I have looked briefly at the dream life of this group of patients and considered the usefulness of this enquiry in a routine interview.

A preoccupation with bowel function and, accordingly, of laxatives, was found in over fifty per cent of these patients and was seen to be far higher rates than a comparable group of elderly in-patients with physical illnesses. In an attempt to understand the meaning of this, the material is viewed from Kleinian and Freudian perspectives.

An additional prescription anomaly compared with the geriatric group was the use of Hypromellose, a conjunctival lubricant known as “artificial tears”, to the patients. The meaning of tears and weeping in psychoanalytic literature is described with this observation in mind.

responseSet.title: A Psychiatric Trainee's Experience of Holding the Burden
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychiatric Trainee's Experience of Holding the Burden

Basant Puri

Basant Puri

Hills Road, Cambridge CB2 2QQ

Basant Puri  

This paper is the description of a piece of clinical material which profoundly worried and upset me in the middle of the second psychotherapy case which formed part of my psychiatric training. It was an understanding of this which led to insight for the patient.

responseSet.documentRef: Wolff, H. (1988) An Outline of Psychotherapy for Medical Students and Practitioners edited by Harold Maxwell. Bristol: Wright. Pp. xv + 107. £6.75 paperback.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:277-277
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Wolff, H. (1988) An Outline of Psychotherapy for Medical Students and Practitioners edited by Harold Maxwell. Bristol: Wright. Pp. xv + 107. £6.75 paperback.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:277-277
responseSet.documentRefXML: Wolff, H. (1988) An Outline of Psychotherapy for Medical Students and Practitioners edited by Harold Maxwell. Bristol: Wright. Pp. xv + 107. £6.75 paperback.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:277-277
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1988 3 3 277-277 An Outline of Psychotherapy for Medical Students and Practitioners edited by Harold Maxwell. Bristol: Wright. Pp. xv + 107. £6.75 paperback. Heinz Wolff
responseSet.title: An Outline of Psychotherapy for Medical Students and Practitioners edited by Harold Maxwell. Bristol: Wright. Pp. xv + 107. £6.75 paperback.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Outline of Psychotherapy for Medical Students and Practitioners edited by Harold Maxwell. Bristol: Wright. Pp. xv + 107. £6.75 paperback.

Heinz Wolff

Heinz Wolff

Heinz Wolff  

The teaching of the basic concepts and practice of psychotherapy to students in medical schools is, with rare exceptions, limited to a few theoretical seminars. As Professor Steven Hirsch, in the foreword to this volume, and the editor Dr Harold Maxwell, in his introductory chapter, point out, this is unfortunate in view of the growing awareness, both within and outside the medical profession, of the important role of the psychological aspects of medical practice and of the doctor-patient relationship. This short and easily readable book is intended to fill this gap.

It consists of eight chapters written by different authors. The editor briefly describes the basic principles of individual psychotherapy, both supportive and insight-directed, and provides a short summary of a few major psychoanalytic concepts. This is followed by a more detailed and helpful account of the history, present-day concepts and clinical applications of psychodynamic psychotherapy, written by David Alexander from the University of Aberdeen. A short contribution by Jon Sklar from Cambridge draws attention to the varied, and sometimes disturbing, feelings students may experience when interviewing and examining patients. He emphasizes the need for students to become sensitive to the meaning of their own feelings and of physical and psychological processes in the practice of medicine. The application of psychological understanding and of psychotherapy to the practice of liaison-psychiatry in a hospital setting is described by Chris Thomas from Leicester Royal Infirmary, special attention being paid to the management of the patient's reactions to his illness.

Perhaps the most practical and lively chapter is the one by Andrew Elder on psychotherapy in general practice. Several brief but highly instructive case histories are given to illustrate important issues concerning the doctor-patient relationship, the nature of diagnosis and the various kinds of psychotherapy that can be used in general practice. This chapter also includes a historical account of the influence of Michael and Enid Balint and of Balint groups on general practice.

responseSet.documentRef: Harrow, A. (1988) From Safety to Superego by Joseph Sandler. London: Karnac. Pp. xix + 348. £14.95.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:282-282
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Harrow, A. (1988) From Safety to Superego by Joseph Sandler. London: Karnac. Pp. xix + 348. £14.95.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:282-282
responseSet.documentRefXML: Harrow, A. (1988) From Safety to Superego by Joseph Sandler. London: Karnac. Pp. xix + 348. £14.95.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:282-282
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1988 3 3 282-282 From Safety to Superego by Joseph Sandler. London: Karnac. Pp. xix + 348. £14.95. Alan Harrow
responseSet.title: From Safety to Superego by Joseph Sandler. London: Karnac. Pp. xix + 348. £14.95.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Safety to Superego by Joseph Sandler. London: Karnac. Pp. xix + 348. £14.95.

Alan Harrow

Alan Harrow

Alan Harrow  

In the foreword to this book, Wallerstein describes Professor Sandler's contribution to psychoanalysis under two rubrics, methodological and conceptual. One facet is the scholarly research enterprise by which all science tests its hypotheses and accrues new empirical knowledge. The other, the theory building enterprise that generates new hypotheses to be tested for their fit with clinical findings. This is both a complementary and concise summary of Sandler as a theoretician whose work is firmly set within the Freudian psychoanalytic mainstream.

Within this volume of twenty-one papers, some with other authors, Sandler has comprehensively brought together work carried out over more than twenty years at the Anna Freud Centre. The various chapters describe the work which has emerged from the research project known as the Hampstead psychoanalytic index. The process of indexing is described in the appendix. (The pilot study involved indexing psychoanalytic material of fifty children in five-times-a-week analysis.) Beginning with a paper first published by Sandler in 1959 titled ‘The background of safety’, perhaps one of his most original contributions to psychoanalytic theory, he introduces the concept of ‘safety’ and the associated ‘safety principle’. The latter he suggests mediates the development of the reality principle from the pleasure principle. While he refrains from developing this notion in detail, he does describe at length the concept of safety which he believes develops from an integral part of primary narcisstic experience which exists in rudimentary form from the time of the earliest experiences of need satisfaction. Constant reference is made to this illuminating paper in later chapters.

In the third chapter there is Sandler's paper on ‘The concept of the superego’, which was the first major piece of research reported from the index project. Building on Freud's concept of the superego, he attempts a theoretical reformulation of it with an emphasis on the more friendly, supporting and loving aspects.

responseSet.documentRef: Whyte, R. (1988) ‘Why Psychotherapy?’ by Nini Herman. London: Free Association Books. p. 165.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:178-179
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Whyte, R. (1988) ‘Why Psychotherapy?’ by Nini Herman. London: Free Association Books. p. 165.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:178-179
responseSet.documentRefXML: Whyte, R. (1988) ‘Why Psychotherapy?’ by Nini Herman. London: Free Association Books. p. 165.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:178-179
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1988 3 2 178-179 ‘Why Psychotherapy?’ by Nini Herman. London: Free Association Books. p. 165. Robert Whyte
responseSet.title: ‘Why Psychotherapy?’ by Nini Herman. London: Free Association Books. p. 165.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Why Psychotherapy?’ by Nini Herman. London: Free Association Books. p. 165.

Robert Whyte

Robert Whyte

Robert Whyte  

Herman is a psychotherapist who spent many years as a general medical practioner before coming to psychotherapy training via psychiatry. With this background, having been confronted by intractable problems of neurosis in her patients, and then having made the decision to do further training to be able to tackle these problems constructively, she should be in a good position to answer the question, “Why Psychotherapy?” Unfortunately, her experience of psychiatry seems to have been a bad one (“a distressing, painful sentence”). This means that her answer may not be generally applicable.

In a slim volume, she manages to give an understandable and interesting account of the history and development of psychoanalytic theories, particularly of Freud, Klein and Bion. In this framework, she gives an account of the development, healthy and otherwise, of the individual. Brief accounts of some of Freud's and Klein's case histories are included. A chapter entitled “The Opposition's Case” is devoted to looking at opposition to psychotherapy, mainly from psychiatry. Herman considers the opposition to arise mainly from envy, the death instinct, and unwillingness to look at the needy part within the self. She discusses the origins of life in the first relationship, and work as a reparative act, before moving on to consider, at some length, the war between the sexes and to praise the development to the full of the bisexual nature within each individual. The final chapter, entitled “Why Psychotherapy?”, starts with another attack on psychiatry and patients who seek psychiatric help. Herman tries, with some success, to link physical illness and emotional states, giving AIDS as one example.

178

responseSet.title: The Difficulty of Understanding a Borderline Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Difficulty of Understanding a Borderline Patient

Rahle Issroff

Rahle Issroff

5 Acacia Road
London, NW8 6AB

Rahle Issroff  

For much of the time during my work with Mr A, a borderline patient whom I saw for twice-weekly psychotherapy over a period of one year. I had difficulty understanding and communicating with him. I felt despairing and impotent and that therapy was not progressing at all. A few weeks prior to termination a shift occurred and I realised that there had been some improvement in Mr A's functioning. Although I was aware that behind the polite façade was an angry, disturbed man, I felt that he was a little more in touch with reality than he had been at the beginning of the treatment.

In this paper I will focus on the factors that contributed to my difficulty in understanding and communicating with Mr A. I will also attempt to account for his apparent improvement.

responseSet.title: Smiling, Swallowing, Sickening and Stupefying: The Effect of Sexual Abuse on the Child
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Smiling, Swallowing, Sickening and Stupefying: The Effect of Sexual Abuse on the Child

Valerie Sinason

Valerie Sinason

120 Belsize Lane
London NW3 5BA.

Valerie Sinason  

Referrals for child sexual abuse have increased dramatically in the UK, Europe and America. As assessment skills improve we find earlier ages at onset of abuse. Referrals of mentally and multiply handicapped children who have been abused are also increasing.

This paper tries to understand the meaning of abuse and its effect on the child by examining the process of small everyday abuse in a loving home. This is done by means of extracts from a baby observation. The infantile processes of smiling, swallowing, being sick and becoming stupid are then traced in the twice-weekly psychoanalytical psychotherapy of two children, a boy and a girl. The trauma of sexual abuse played a part in the mental handicap of the boy who is seen here moving from tragic victim of abuse to adolescent struggling against abusing. The girl is 5 and abuse has led to her emotional and learning difficulties.

responseSet.title: Psychodynamic Aspects of Paedophilia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Aspects of Paedophilia

Mervin Classer

Mervin Classer

8 Fitzjohn's Avenue
NW3 5NA

Mervin Classer  

Paedophilia is distinguished from incest. The classification of paedophilia into ‘Invariant’ and ‘Pseudo-Neurotic’ types is made and clinically illustrated. Some features of the psychopathological structure which paedophilia has in common with other perversions are described and the paper goes on to consider the influences these features have on the paedophiles’ internal and external object-relating and their detrimental effects on the paedophiles’ formative psychic processes and consequent character structure.

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1988 3 2 176-178 Workbooks for Psychotherapists by Robert Langs M.D. Vol. 1. Understanding Unconscious Communication. £18.00. Pp 132. Vol. 2. Listening and Formulating. £23.00. Pp 292. Vol. 3. Intervening & Validating. £28.50. Pp 277. New Concept Press. Michael Miller
responseSet.title: Workbooks for Psychotherapists by Robert Langs M.D. Vol. 1. Understanding Unconscious Communication. £18.00. Pp 132. Vol. 2. Listening and Formulating. £23.00. Pp 292. Vol. 3. Intervening & Validating. £28.50. Pp 277. New Concept Press.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Workbooks for Psychotherapists by Robert Langs M.D. Vol. 1. Understanding Unconscious Communication. £18.00. Pp 132. Vol. 2. Listening and Formulating. £23.00. Pp 292. Vol. 3. Intervening & Validating. £28.50. Pp 277. New Concept Press.

Michael Miller

Michael Miller

Michael Miller  

These Work Books for Psychotherapists contain a distillation of Robert Langs’ “communicative approach” to understanding the therapist/patient relationship. Since 1971, when the central concept was outlined in a paper in the American Journal of Psychoanalysis (Volume 19), Langs has developed and elaborated his approach in many volumes, quoting verbatim supervisory material as illustration.

These present volumes again rely on extensive case material, but differ in as much as the reader is invited to record his responses on the blank pages provided, in what are heralded as the first active learning books in psychotherapy. Langs’ approach relies on a structure derived from two main sources whose application to therapy is to illuminate the relationship as primarily interactive and adaptive. Freud's concept of the dream, with particular emphasis on communication between the unconscious and preconscious, through derivatives, joins forces with a definition of transference derived from Melanie Klein.

What Langs wishes to emphasize is the apparent discrepancy he feels exists between the theoretical role of unconscious process in psychotherapy and what seems to be the case clinically. He begins with the assumption that with the exception of major external trauma, everything expressed by a patient in psychotherapy is directed towards, or is about, the therapist. Langs argues that although the “transference relationship” is regarded almost universally by psychotherapists as crucial to the therapeutic endeavour, when it comes to its clinical interpretation, it is more often than not done by utilizing the most obvious surface implications of the patient's material. At best, this means that whilst the patient's attention is drawn to the unconscious displacement of the fantasy, the content is taken at its face value. Langs concludes that although the therapist might imagine that he is dealing with unconscious process, this is not borne out by careful study of the patient's responses after such interventions.

responseSet.title: The Constitution and Operation of the Super Ego
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Constitution and Operation of the Super Ego

Ruth Malcolm

Ruth Malcolm

33 Greenhill
London NW3 5UA

Ruth Malcolm  

This paper deals with the history and development of the superego, that is the evolution of the internal objects from birth through infancy and childhood. It describes how primitive emotions, both negative and positive, leave a mark in the superego, remaining as the potentiality that can be reactivated. In my view the primitive and cruel aspects of the superego derive from very early frightening objects, while the ego ideal on the contrary, or the ideal of the self derive from very early idealised objects. I give two clinical examples, one to illustrate how the objects do not change and the second to show the operation and changes in the superego in the transference.

responseSet.title: Splitting and Stupidity in Child Sexual Abuse
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Splitting and Stupidity in Child Sexual Abuse

Sebastian Kraemer

Sebastian Kraemer

London NW3 5BA

Sebastian Kraemer  

One of the functions of “the taboo against incest” is to prevent discussion of the subject — an ancient cover up. The discovery that sexual abuse is occurring on afar wider scale than was previously believed has divided society in two. In particular cases where abuse is known or suspected there is a similar partition of opinion, both within the minds of the individuals involved and between them, inducing a kind of stupidity which undermines the capacity for honesty and judgement, and all but the most authentic professional skills.

responseSet.documentRef: Coles, P. (1988) Aspects of Perversion in Anorexic/Bulimic Disorders. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 3:137-147

responseSet.title: Aspects of Perversion in Anorexic/Bulimic Disorders
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aspects of Perversion in Anorexic/Bulimic Disorders

Prophecy Coles

Prophecy Coles

18 Cheyne Row
London SW3 5HL

Prophecy Coles  

In this paper I have suggested that in the cases of anorexia and bulimia that I have treated there seems to be a sado-masochistic structure similar to that which I observed in the case of a transvestite. In the anorexic and bulimic behaviour there seemed to be an omnipotent and compulsive use of the body as a phallic object. This use of the body, through bulimia and vomiting, or in self-starvation, with its attendant loss of feminine characteristics such as breasts, rounded buttocks and menstruation, was related to phantasies about castration. Femininity was being denied while at the same time it was secretly preserved as an exciting phallic object. It is this characteristic that brings it close to the perverse acting out of the transvestite, who denied his masculinity while dressed as a woman, while at the same time preserving his excited and erect penis.

responseSet.title: Omnipotence and Idealization as Contributory Factors in a Failure to Mourn
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Omnipotence and Idealization as Contributory Factors in a Failure to Mourn

Melinda Schneider

Melinda Schneider

120 Belsize Lane
London NW3 5BA

Melinda Schneider  

In this paper some clinical material from work with a patient who had been unable to mourn his mother's death is described. Freud's papers on loss (Freud, 1917; 1926) and Melanie Klein's theory of early emotional development provide the theoretical framework for the discussion of the clinical material. Particular emphasis is given to Freud's concept of mental pain (Freud, 1926) and Klein's description of primitive defences against unbearable pain and anxiety. The essential function of the primary objects, in helping to make the anxiety and mental pain associated with loss more bearable, is also discussed.
